coredata.dc:title: Green and efficient synthesis of carbon quantum dots and their luminescent properties
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Langfeng
Qiao, Meng
Zhang, Lei
Sun, Lu
Zhang, Yang
Liu, Weiwei
coredata.dc:description: Using citric acid as the carbon source and glutathione as nitrogen and sulfur donor, carbon quantum dots co-doped with N and S (N/S–CQDs) were successfully synthesized with a fast (within 1 h) and clean microwave-assisted oil bath method. The quantum yield reached as high as 76.5% at 350 nm excitation light. Simultaneously, we found that the N/S–CQDs were featured with good anti-photobleaching properties and sodium chloride concentration had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CQDs, which were of great significance for the application of fluorescent N/S-CQDs in real environment such as sensing and imaging. Moreover, N/S-CQDs coped gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) performed fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from N/S-CQDs to AuNPs, building a complex fluorescent probe for the detection of drug methimazole.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of external conditions on luminescence intensity of fluorescence N/S-CQDs
Detection of methimazole with N/S-CQDs and AuNPs complex fluorescent probes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by The National Key Research and Development Program ( 2018YFB0504400 ); Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin ( 16JCQNJC05100 ); Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources ( Guangxi Normal University )(CMEMR2016-B11); National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 11574160 ); National Basic Research Program of China ( 2014CB339802 ); Special Support Plan for Talent Development in Tianjin ; 111 Project ( B16027 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities . W.L. acknowledges the support of the open research funds of the State Key Laboratory of High Field Laser Physics, Shanghai Institute of Optics and Fine Mechanics (SIOM)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the obtained quantum dots. (a) Solution and Dried N/S-CQDs, (b)TEM image, (c) particle size distribution of N/S-CQDs and (d) FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs and N/S-CQDs.
(a)Excitation and emission spectrum of N/S-CQDs and (b) the integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance values of quinine sulfate and N/S-CQDs.

(a)Fluorescence of N/S-CQDs with different irradiation time and fluorescence peak intensity changes with time (as shown in the inset). and (b) emission spectrum of N/S-CQDs at different excitation wavelength.

(a). Fluorescence intensity of N/S-CQDs and the change trend graph of fluorescence peak intensity (as shown in the inset) at different pH values. and (b) fluorescence intensity of N/S-CQDs and its peak intensity change trend graph (as shown in the inset) at different salt concentrations.

Fluorescence spectrum of N/S-CQDs, N/S-CQDs+AuNPs, and N/S-CQDs+AuNPs+methimazole. The concentrations of N/S-CQD, AuNP and methimazole applied in this detection were 114 μg/ml, 0.45 nM, 44 μg/ml, respectively.

head.ce:title: Green and efficient synthesis of carbon quantum dots and their luminescent properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Modern Optics, Nankai University, and Tianjin Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Sensor and Sensing Network Technology, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Modern Optics, Nankai University, and Tianjin Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Sensor and Sensing Network Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, Guangxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Contributed equally to the work presented here and should therefore be regarded as equivalent authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using citric acid as the carbon source and glutathione as nitrogen and sulfur donor, carbon quantum dots co-doped with N and S (N/S–CQDs) were successfully synthesized with a fast (within 1 h) and clean microwave-assisted oil bath method. The quantum yield reached as high as 76.5% at 350 nm excitation light. Simultaneously, we found that the N/S–CQDs were featured with good anti-photobleaching properties and sodium chloride concentration had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CQDs, which were of great significance for the application of fluorescent N/S-CQDs in real environment such as sensing and imaging. Moreover, N/S-CQDs coped gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) performed fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from N/S-CQDs to AuNPs, building a complex fluorescent probe for the detection of drug methimazole.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dot (CQD) is a type of luminescent materials whose particle size is within 1–100 nm. This type of particles are symbolized with big surface curvature and high specific surface area, which results in nano effect [1] such as the surface effect, the quantum size effect, the macro tunnel quantum effect, etc. Therefore, CQDs show better performance than bulk materials in terms of photoluminescence properties [2], photocatalytic properties [3], nonlinear optical properties [4], which make CQDs highly potent in the applications of sensing, photocatalysis, imaging and optical storage. For example, CQDs have be used to build fluorescent probe for the detection of iron ion and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs shows a good linear response to the concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 0–300 μmol L−1 (R2 = 0.99) with a detection limit of 13.68 μmol L−1 [5].

A simple, safe, fast and green synthetic approach of CQDs will be welcome in practice. Currently, numerous approaches have been reported to synthesize CQDs with raw materials taken from natural materials such as single-walled carbon nanotubes [6], chitosan [7] and polyethylene glycol [8] to cabbage leaves [9], straw [10] and orange juice [11]. Arch discharge technique was the first one used to synthesize CQDs during the preparation of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) by Xu et.al. [12]. But the arc-discharge SWNTs were impure and all of the fluorescent components took up only 10% of the mass of the SWNT suspension. These materials fluoresced at green-blue, yellow, and orange wavelength. The quantum yield of the yellow fluorescent material which was measured at 366 nm excitation was found as low as 0.016. In 2009, Liu et al. reported a straightforward route towards nano-sized (1.5–2.5 nm) carbon dots (CDs) with amorphous structure by employing surfactant-modified silica spheres as carriers [13]. First, the surface of the nanosilica sphere was functionalized with a copolymer F127 that contained both hydrophilic segments and hydrophobic segments. Subsequently, the molecular weight of less than 500 phenolic copolymer as carbon precursors, under nitrogen atmosphere, high temperature pyrolysis CQDs and SiO2 composite system. By pyrolysis, silica were removed and CQDs were thereafter obtained. Finally, after acidification and surface passivation, fluorescent CQDs with good water solubility were obtained. However, the quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized CQDs could only reach 14.7% and high temperature condition was required in the synthesis process. In view of the shortcomings of previous synthetic methods such as time-consuming, laborious and demanding conditions, Zhu et al. [14] synthesized CQDs with photoluminescent properties using domestic microwave ovens so that the synthesis of fluorescent CQDs opened up the opportunity to move from the laboratory to the actual batch produce. Nevertheless, the observed QY remained to be improved. Similarly, some researchers used ascorbic acid vitamin C as a carbon source and alanine as a nitrogen source to synthesize fluorescent N -CQDs using the energy generated by the instantaneous microwave [15]. But the quantum yield of synthesized N-CDs was 14% against the reference quinine sulfate and its dialysis process was time-consuming (more than 1 day). In 2013, Dong et.al. [16] synthesized fluorescent CQDs with 29.5 mM citric acid as carbon source and 8.3 mM cysteine as nitrogen source and sulfur source, which accomplished a much higher QY of 73%. Notably, the experimental process took a relatively long time (more than 15 h). Water-soluble carbon quantum dots have been simply prepared by microwave treatment of lysine as a building block within 5 min in household microwave oven. Nevertheless, the luminescence was still weak with a low quantum efficiency [17].

It is of significance to find out a fast and convenient way to prepare fluorescent CQDs with excellent optical properties for sensing and imaging. However, most of the synthetic methods have been limited by expensive raw materials, complex operation or low QY. In light of the mentioned requirements, we hereby introduce a novel method which applies citric acid as a carbon source and glutathione as nitrogen and sulfur source to synthesize nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N/S-CQDs) by microwave-assisted oil bath, which is simple, safe and efficient. The luminescence property of the synthesized N/S-CQDs has been proved to be competent for practical usage. Moreover, we utilized the synthesized fluorescent N/S-CQDs as energy donors and AuNPs as energy acceptors to construct a complex fluorescent probe, which demonstrated its applicability in the detection of drug methimazole.

First, we used citric acid as a carbon source and ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source to synthesize carbon quantum dots co-doped with nitrogen (N-CQDs) through a microwave-assisted oil bath method. Its quantum yield reaches 20.3%. In order to further obtain high QY fluorescent carbon quantum dots, we synthesized carbon quantum dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur (N/S–CQDs). The synthetic procedure is as follows: Citric acid (C6H8O7, analytical grade, 1 g) and glutathione (C10H17N3O6S, analytical grade, 0.1 g) were mixed in 20 ml ultrapure water and stirred to disperse evenly. The mixed solution of citric acid and glutathione was then placed in a conical flask which was connected to the condenser tube. Thereafter, we placed it in a microwave oven for 10 min, during which the output power of microwave oven is set to 800 W at 2450 MHz frequency. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was placed in a 200 °C oil bath pot to heat for 10 min before removal. The obtained fluorescent N / S - CQDs were placed in a 2000 D dialysis bag and dialyzed 4 times for every 2 h.

QY is expressed by Φ, where Φ ≤ 1. We hereby chose quinine sulfate as the reference in this work, in which 10 mg quinine sulfate solid was dissolved with 0.1 M sulfuric acid and dilute to the desired concentration. It is known that the QY of the reference substance quinine sulfate is 0.54 [18] under excitation at a wavelength of 350 nm. The QY of the obtained CQD can be calculated with the following formula:
(2.1)
ϕ =
ϕ
R
×
I
I
R
×
A
R
A
×
η
2
η
R
2
where φ represents QY, I represents the integrated fluorescence intensity of the emission spectrum, and A represents the absorbance of the incident light at the same incident wavelength, η represents the refractive index of the solvent and the subscript R represents the reference substance. The solvent used in this work was water and sulfuric acid, whose refractive index was 1.33 and could be eliminated accordingly. We have measured the absorbance of five groups with different concentrations to achieve the corresponding fluorescence integral intensity, and the above formula is abbreviated as
(2.2)
ϕ =
ϕ
R
×
G r a d
G r a
d
R
in which Grad stands for I/A and can be fitted through the measured QY at different concentration.

The absorbance A (required A < 0.1) of quinine sulfate at different concentrations was measured with an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer for more than 5 groups. Thereafter, the emission spectra of five groups of quinine sulfate solution were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer at 350 nm excitation light, and the corresponding integrated fluorescence intensity was calculated. Then the absorbance values and corresponding integrated fluorescence intensities of five different concentrations of fluorescence N/S-CQDs were measured.

The obtained quantum dots solution is found to be light yellow and could be dried into gel in Fig. 1(a). The TEM image of the N/S-CQDs is displayed in Fig. 1(b), it can be seen that the obtained quantum dots are at the scale of nanometer and they are sparsely distributed, which indicates the good solubility in water. Through the size diagram in Fig. 1(c), we find that 90% of the N/S-CQDs are within the range of 6–14 nm and the distribution at this range is even.

The synthesized N/S-CQDs were further characterized with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. In the case of FTIR, we obtained a gelatinous yellow solid by lyophilization, as shown in Fig. 1(a). The gelatinous solid was further mixed evenly with dried KBr powder and compressed into a translucent tablet. Then the infrared spectrum of N/S-CQDs was measured with the pure KBr as the background. The infrared spectra of the fluorescent N-CQDs and N/S-CQDs are shown in Fig. 1(d). The characteristic absorption peaks of N-CQDs are found at the 3500–3000 cm-1 wavenumber range correspond to the stretching vibration absorption peaks of carboxyl and amino groups. The characteristic absorption peak at 1436 cm-1 corresponds to the vibration and deformation of N-H, which infers to the presence of amino groups. The characteristic absorption peak at 1653 cm-1 indicates the presence of carbonyl. The above data shows that there are carboxyl and amino functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs.

For N/S-CQDs,the characteristic absorption peak at 3496 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration absorption peak of the carboxyl group. The absorption peak at 3290 cm−1 and 1426 cm−1 corresponding to the vibrations and deformations of the N-H groups, which characterizes the presence of amino functional groups. The small peak at 2572 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration absorption peak of the thiol group, which we do not find in the FTIR of N-CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 2(a), N/S-CQDs are blue-emitting under UV lamp (as shown in the inset). The maximum excitation wavelength is located at 350 nm and the maximum emission wavelength is 421 nm. The Stokes displacement is as high as 71 nm. Such a large Stokes displacement can avoid the interference of excitation light and provides a good condition for the practical application such as fluorescent dyes of N/S-CQDs. Fluorescent N/S-CQDs have been synthesized with hydrothermal method and microwave assisted oil bath and the quantum yields at different concentrations are shown in Fig. 2(b). It is found that the gradient of fluorescence N/S-CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method and microwave - assisted oil bath method are larger than quinine sulfate. From formula 2.2, it is notably that the higher the slope, the higher the QY. Moreover, the exact QY value can be fitted according to Fig. 2(b) with the gradients and the QY of quinine sulfate. The QY of fluorescence N/S-CQDs synthesized by microwave-assisted oil bath is 76.5%, while the QY of fluorescent N/S-CQDs synthesized with hydrothermal method is 65.4% under the same conditions. The N/S-CQDs obtained with microwave assisted oil bath have better fluorescence properties than not only quinine sulfate but also CQDs prepared with hydrothermal method. Furthermore, the microwave assisted oil bath method are far more ready to use since the hydrothermal method requires high temperature and high pressure.

Besides the efficacy to luminesce, the durability and stability in the practical application are other concerns need to be attended. We further measured the photobleaching performance of N/S-CQDs. The width of the incident slit and the exit slit was set at 3 nm, and the irradiation was continued under the excitation of 350 nm excitation light. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the fluorescence spectra of N/S-CQDs hardly changed for 30 min under the excitation light. The inset in Fig. 3(a) shows more clearly fluorescence peak intensity changes a little with the time of excitation light irradiation. The fluorescence intensity is found to be 256 a.u. and the value stays above 249 a.u. after 30 min of excitation. The deviation of the intensity remains under 2%. The excellent anti-photobleaching property of fluorescent N/S-CQDs guarantees the durability of luminescence and is of significance for the precision as a fluorescent probe.

Furthermore, the emission wavelength of the fluorescence N/S-CQDs synthesized by the microwave-assisted oil bath method does not change with the excitation wavelength with the emission spectrum of N/S-CQDs displayed Fig. 3(b). As the excitation wavelength increased from 320 nm to 380 nm, the emission wavelength of quantum dot was stable at 421 nm. This characteristic may come from doping sulfur elements owing to its ability to suppress excitation-dependent photo-luminescence, making the luminescence of N/S-CQD different from that of the N-CQD shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information) [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of external conditions on luminescence intensity of fluorescence N/S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In bio-detection applications, the CQD should also have stable optical properties under various external conditions. In most cases, the main influencing factor is the pH and the salinity of the detecting samples. We investigated the fluorescence properties of co-doped N/S-CQDs in two aspects: acidity and alkalinity, salt tolerance. We adjusted the aqueous solution containing fluorescent N/S-CQDs to alter the pH values with 1.0 M dilute hydrochloric acid and 1.0 M sodium hydroxide. The effect of acid-base of the external environment on the luminescent properties of fluorescent N/S-CQDs was obtained by fluorescence spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the peak of the fluorescence N/S-CQDs emission spectrum is 421 nm when pH equal to 7. After adding sodium hydroxide, the pH value increased but the luminescence peak remain the same, indicating the chemical-stability of the N/S-CQDs. Moreover, the intensity of the emitted light had only minor changes, where the fluorescence peak intensity of the emission was slightly increased in the alkaline environment. The change of the tendency of fluorescence intensity along with pH is shown in inset of Fig. 4(a). We found the maximum intensity is found as 251 a.u. when pH is 10, while the minimum value as is 223 a.u. It can be seen that the pH value of the external environment can only influence the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CQDs as small as 10%.(With the increase of pH value, the intensity of the emitted light almost remains unchanged in the acidic and neutral environment, the fluorescence intensity slightly increases in the alkaline environment, the peak position of the emission spectrum remains unchanged).

Besides, the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CQDs is also hardly affected by NaCl concentration. As shown in the results of Fig. 4(b), the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CQDs is slightly increased from 235 a.u. to 256 a.u., i.e. less than 10%, with the NaCl concentration increased from 0.0 M to 1.0 M. The inset in Fig. 4(b) shows the change in fluorescence peak intensity of N/S-CQDs with NaCl concentration indicating that N/S-CQDs have good salt resistance. That the acidity and salinity affects little is of vital importance for the in vitro and even in vivo bio-detection applying N/S-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of methimazole with N/S-CQDs and AuNPs complex fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: We further applied the synthesized N/S-CQDs as drug probe in vitro to test the efficacy in practical usage. Methimazole is an antithyroid drug that reduces thyroid hormone synthesis and alleviates immune disorders. However, overdose of methimazole can cause harm to the human body. Current methods for the determination of methimazole include spectrophotometry [20], supercritical fluid chromatography [21], chemiluminescence [22] and several other common methods. However, these methods also have inherent disadvantages, such as high cost and complicated operation. N/S-CQDs are found to construct complex fluorescent probes together with AuNPs owing to the presence of –SH group in quantum dots. Moreover, methimazole is detected by FRET between fluorescent N/S-CQDs and AuNPs, with the FRET spectra shown in Fig. S2(Supporting Information).

The luminescence spectrum is shown in the Fig. 5. As we expected, adding AuNPs to the fluorescent N/S-CQDs solution resulted in a sharp drop in the fluorescence intensity (from 161 to 99) of N/S-CQDs due to the FRET effect. Nevertheless, when methimazole is first added to the AuNPs solution, the sulfhydryl (-SH) of the methimazole will bind to the AuNPs and coat the surface of the AuNPs to prevent FRET between N/S-CQDs and AuNPs. The fluorescence of N/S-CQDs is not completely quenched, and fluorescence intensity is restored, in this case the number is maintained at 117.

In summary, an economic, simple, green, fast and safe method has been successfully developed for the synthesis of N/S-CQDs through microwave-assisted oil bath, This approach applied low-cost raw materials(citric acid glutathione) and was featured with short reaction time(1 h) and convenient post-processing. Strong luminescence was observed for the synthesized CQDs with the quantum yield as high as 76.5% and a big Stokes displacement (nm). Moreover, the CQDs were found stable under various conditions with the luminescence peak remained constant after long-term excitation or under different acidity and salinity. Fluorescence intensity of N/S-CQDs changed little after 30 min luminescence or with increasing pH and NaCl concentration. The advantage of the stable structure and good luminescence property are of great significance for the application of bio-detection. Finally, we demonstrate that the efficacy of the synthesized fluorescent N/S-CQDs in drug detection and methimazole has been detected by affecting the FRET between N/S-CQD and AuNP. This synthetic approach which produces CQD with excellent optical property is highly promising for the industrialization and application of quantum dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by The National Key Research and Development Program (2018YFB0504400); Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin (16JCQNJC05100); Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Guangxi Normal University)(CMEMR2016-B11); National Natural Science Foundation of China (11574160); National Basic Research Program of China (2014CB339802); Special Support Plan for Talent Development in Tianjin; 111 Project (B16027) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities. W.L. acknowledges the support of the open research funds of the State Key Laboratory of High Field Laser Physics, Shanghai Institute of Optics and Fine Mechanics (SIOM)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.10.057.
